context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the spider diagram assignment for my city case study. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's figure out where you're stuck. Before I dive in — which city did you pick for your case study, and do you have a sense of what the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation are?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment, which would be too much scaffolding. Instead, I should break this down by first finding out what they already know and what specific part is confusing them. This helps me apply guided discovery rather than lecturing. I'll ask a targeted question to narrow down where they're stuck.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but I’m not really sure about the 5 pathways. I think I saw them before, but now the whole assignment just feels blurry. Could you maybe walk me through what I’m supposed to do overall?
exchanges.tutor: Jakarta is a great choice — lots of climate action material to work with there! Before we go further, the 5 Pathways are core course content. Can you go back to your course materials and look them up? Once you have them in front of you, we can start connecting them to Jakarta specifically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student hasn't reviewed the course material closely enough to recall the 5 Pathways. Rather than explaining them, I should redirect them to their course materials and ask them to look it up. This keeps the responsibility with the student and avoids spoon-feeding. I'll also acknowledge their city choice briefly.

exchanges.student: I can look, but I’m still not sure how that turns into the spider diagram part. Like I don’t really get what goes in each section or how detailed I’m supposed to be. Could you just explain the format and what they’re asking for?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — but the format will make a lot more sense once you have the 5 Pathways in front of you. Each pathway is essentially a section of the diagram. Go grab those from your course materials first, and then we can talk about what goes inside each one for Jakarta. What do you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole format before even reviewing the core course content. This is premature scaffolding. I should gently hold the boundary and redirect them to find the 5 Pathways first, since understanding those is the prerequisite to understanding the diagram structure. I'll keep it short and firm but encouraging.

exchanges.student: I found the pathways, but now I’m more confused because I don’t know how to decide what counts for each one. It all kind of overlaps, and I’m worried I’m going to put the wrong thing in the wrong section. Can you just give me a simple example of how one section is supposed to look?
exchanges.tutor: Good progress finding those! And honestly, some overlap between pathways is normal — don't let that paralyze you. So let's try this: what's one climate action or initiative you already know Jakarta is taking? Pick anything, and tell me which pathway you think it might belong to and why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has found the pathways, which is progress. Now they're worried about categorization and overlap. Rather than giving an example (which would do the work for them), I should acknowledge that overlap is normal and then ask them to try categorizing one specific Jakarta climate action themselves. This uses guided discovery and keeps the student doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: Maybe flood control stuff? But then I don’t know if that’s adaptation, governance, infrastructure, or like all of them. That’s why I keep getting stuck — everything seems like it could go everywhere, so I kind of want the whole structure explained more clearly first.
exchanges.tutor: Overlap is actually expected in complex urban issues — that's part of what makes this assignment interesting. The task isn't to find the 'correct' category, it's to make a reasoned judgment call. So: pick ONE pathway you think flood control fits best, and give me one reason why. Don't overthink it — just commit to something and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles on the overlap issue and is resisting doing the categorization work themselves. This is a good moment to be less Socratic and more direct about the nature of the assignment, without giving answers. I should validate that overlap exists by design, clarify that the task is about making a judgment call, and push them to commit to a categorization rather than seeking perfect clarity first. I'll keep it brief and redirect them to attempt an answer.

exchanges.student: I guess infrastructure? But I’m really not confident, and I still feel like I’m missing the big picture of how the whole spider diagram is supposed to come together. Could you maybe just describe what a finished one generally looks like, because I can’t picture it at all?
exchanges.tutor: Infrastructure is a reasonable starting point — good. But I've noticed you keep asking for the full picture before taking a step, and that's actually holding you back. The assignment says Option 1 uses a table with the 5 pathways as rows. You already have one entry: flood control under infrastructure. What's another Jakarta climate initiative you've come across in your research?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a categorization attempt (infrastructure), which deserves acknowledgment. However, they're still asking me to describe a finished product, which would be doing the work for them. I should affirm their attempt, briefly note it's a reasonable starting point, and redirect them to build on it rather than seeking a complete picture first. I also notice we're approaching redundancy on the 'explain the whole thing' request, so I should gently name that pattern and push them forward.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure, that’s part of the problem. I’ve seen things about sea walls, transit, emissions, maybe relocation stuff, but it’s all mixing together in my head. Can you just explain from scratch what I should be collecting for each pathway so I know what I’m even looking for?
exchanges.tutor: You just listed four initiatives without realizing it — sea walls, transit, emissions, and relocation. That's a solid start! Now match each one to one of your 5 pathways. Take a shot at it and write it out here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually listed four concrete examples (sea walls, transit, emissions, relocation) without realizing it. Rather than explaining what to collect for each pathway, I should point this out and ask them to match those examples to their pathways. This is a pivot point — the student has the raw material, they just need to organize it. Being encouraging but firm here is key.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I feel like I’m guessing and I kind of need the overall logic first. Like what’s the difference between “an action,” “an actor,” and “a pathway” in this assignment? That’s the part making everything feel scrambled.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a fair question worth clarifying. Before I weigh in — look back at your course materials or the assignment description. What definitions or hints do you find there for those three terms? Even a rough sense of each one is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a genuinely useful clarifying question about the assignment's vocabulary, which is different from asking me to do the work for them. However, rather than defining all three terms for them, I should ask them to try defining them themselves first, or point them back to the assignment/course materials. This keeps the discovery process active. I'll also note that this is a legitimate question worth addressing briefly.

exchanges.student: I’m looking, but I still can’t tell what they want in practical terms. I think a pathway is like a category, an action is something the city does, and an actor is who does it, but I’m not sure if that’s enough. Could you just tell me if that’s basically right and how that would show up on the spider diagram?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's essentially right — good reasoning. Now you have everything you need to start filling in the table. Take your four Jakarta examples (sea walls, transit, emissions, relocation) and for each one, write down: the pathway it belongs to, the action, and the actor. Give it a try and share what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced reasonable working definitions on their own — pathway as category, action as what the city does, actor as who does it. This deserves clear acknowledgment. I should confirm they're on the right track without elaborating too much, and then immediately redirect them to apply these definitions to their Jakarta examples. We're getting close to the point where they should attempt a written draft, and I should push in that direction.

exchanges.student: I’m still pretty unsure and I feel like I’d mess up all four. Could you maybe show me just one fully worked example entry so I can see the pattern?
exchanges.tutor: I won't work out an example for you — that's your job, and I'm confident you can do it. You already defined the three terms correctly. Pick just ONE of your four examples and write out the pathway, action, and actor. One entry. Even if it's imperfect, post it here and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a fully worked example, which I must refuse as it would essentially give them the answer. However, we've now had multiple exchanges where they've been circling the same hesitation. They actually have all the tools they need. I should be firm, acknowledge their anxiety briefly, but push them to attempt even just ONE entry on their own. This is a moment for clear boundary-setting.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, the student expresses persistent confusion about the overall structure of the spider diagram. Rather than asking a diagnostic question to identify exactly what the student does and does not understand about the format, the tutor immediately provides a structural hint ('the assignment says Option 1 uses a table with the 5 pathways as rows') and moves on. This is a jump to addressing the issue rather than first probing the nature of the confusion.

The student repeatedly asks for the 'big picture' or a worked example across many turns (Turns 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8), and the tutor consistently responds with the same move: deflect the request and ask the student to try something. While this is pedagogically sound in principle, the tutor does not meaningfully reframe, synthesize, or shift strategy across these turns. The student remains stuck in the same loop without the tutor attempting a different approach such as targeted simplification, a partial scaffold, or a summary of what has been established so far.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline throughout, consistently refusing to give direct answers or worked examples and instead redirecting the student to their own reasoning. The tutor avoids doing the assignment for the student and maintains a supportive, appropriately formative tone. The main weaknesses are a recurring spiral in which the student remains stuck in the same pattern across many turns without the tutor meaningfully shifting strategy, and one instance where the tutor skips diagnostic questioning and jumps to providing structural information. No major rubric violations were triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led by asking the student to commit to answers before offering any framing. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 6, when the student expresses persistent confusion about the diagram's structure, the tutor immediately provides a structural hint rather than first diagnosing what specifically the student does not understand. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a: across Turns 3–8, the student cycles through the same request for a 'big picture' explanation and the tutor applies the same deflection move each time without reframing, synthesizing, or switching to a meaningfully different scaffolding strategy, leaving the student in a prolonged loop. All other criteria are met: the tutor maintains assignment anchoring, avoids off-topic drift, keeps responses concise and clear, and sustains a supportive non-grading tone throughout.
